# Figure description: скв_136_и_усл_обозн.jpg

---

## Figure Type

This is a **borehole/well log composite diagram** (Russian: **диаграммы разреза** — "section diagrams"), a standard Soviet-era геолого-геофизический разрез (geological-geophysical section) for a single drillhole. It presents multiple geophysical well-logging curves alongside a lithological column, typical of Soviet exploration drilling documentation from the 1980s.

---

## Coverage and Subject

The figure covers a **single borehole (скважина)** — the specific borehole number is partially visible in the header but largely **[illegible]** at this scan resolution. The title area at the top reads **"ДИАГРАММЫ РАЗРЕЗА"** (Section Diagrams). The log appears to cover a substantial depth interval — visually the log strip spans what appears to be several hundred metres of section, likely in the range of **0–500 m or deeper**, consistent with regional survey drilling in northern Kazakhstan under the M-42/N-42 survey program. Geographic or administrative location details in the header are **[illegible]** at this resolution.

---

## Key Content

The figure is organized as a **multi-track composite well log** running vertically (depth increases downward), with the following observable structure:

- **Left margin columns**: Contain depth scale markings, lithological descriptions, and formation/stratigraphic unit designations — specific text is largely **[illegible]** but column structure is clearly visible with regular row divisions corresponding to depth intervals (likely every 1–5 m or 10 m).

- **Central geophysical curve tracks**: Two to three prominent **wiggly curve traces** run the full length of the log. These show significant variability — high-amplitude oscillations in the upper portion transitioning to lower-amplitude, more subdued responses in the middle sections, with renewed variability toward the lower portion. These are characteristic of:
  - **КС (кажущееся сопротивление)** — apparent electrical resistivity curves
  - **ПС (потенциал самополяризации)** — spontaneous potential (SP) curve
  - Possibly **ГК (гамма-каротаж)** — gamma-ray log
  - The two main central tracks likely represent different electrode spacing configurations (e.g., КС-А2М0.5N and КС-А4М0.5N), standard in Soviet logging practice.

- **Lithological column**: A narrow strip (likely between the depth scale and the geophysical curves) shows **lithological symbols** — patterns indicating rock type changes (sandstone, clay, limestone, etc.) — but individual symbols are **[illegible]** at this resolution.

- **Right-side columns**: Several narrower columns on the right side of the main log appear to contain **tabular data** — possibly core recovery percentages, formation tops, mineralogical notes, or interpreted lithology codes. These are largely **[illegible]**.

- **Amplitude patterns**: The geophysical curves show:
  - **High-amplitude, sharp deflections** in approximately the upper third — suggesting lithological heterogeneity, possible interbedded resistive (carbonate or sandstone) and conductive (clay) layers.
  - **Broader, lower-amplitude fluctuations** in the middle section — possibly more homogeneous or finer-grained sequences.
  - **Renewed sharp variations** toward the base — possibly basement contact or coarser clastic/volcanic intervals.

- **Lower supplementary section**: At the bottom of the image, there is a **separate shorter diagram panel** labeled (partially visible) — possibly **"СВОДНАЯ ДИАГРАММА"** (summary diagram) or a **correlation panel**, showing abbreviated curves and a data table block in the lower right with tabulated values and text in Russian — **[illegible]** at this resolution.

- **No colour** is present — the document is entirely **black and white**, consistent with a photocopied or microfilmed Soviet technical report.

## Research Value

This composite borehole log is the primary **subsurface lithological and petrophysical record** for one of the drillholes from the 1983–1984 regional survey, providing direct measurement of formation properties (resistivity, SP, possibly gamma-ray) against depth. A geologist or AI research assistant would use this to **correlate subsurface stratigraphy**, identify formation tops, and flag intervals of anomalous resistivity or radioactivity that might indicate **mineralized zones, aquifers, or economically significant lithologies** (e.g., evaporites, ore-bearing horizons, or basement contacts). In the context of the M-42/N-42 regional survey, this log contributes to building a **cross-section of the subsurface structure** of northern Kazakhstan and calibrating geophysical interpretations from the gravity and seismic surveys conducted in the same campaign.
